title: First law of thermodynamics: word problems
description: Let's solve some problems to better understand how to apply the equation of the first law of thermodynamics.
topic_path: First law of thermodynamics: word problems

assessment_items.question_content:
A sample of ideal gas in a cylinder loses $140\ \text J$ of heat while the gas molecules gain $144\ \text J$ of internal energy in the process.

**What is the work done by the gas molecules?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text J$
hints:
##Strategy

One form of the first law of thermodynamics reads

$\underbrace {Q}_{\text{Heat added to the system}}=\Delta U+\underbrace{W}_{\text{Work done by the gas}}$

It's basically saying that when we add heat to a gas, it can either be used for expansion (work done in pushing a piston) or for increasing the temperature/kinetic energy of the gas molecules $(\Delta U)$.

##Solution

Here, 

- Heat is lost by the gas, so $Q=-140\ \text J$

- Internal energy is *gained*, so$\Delta U=144\ \text J$

Now let's input these into the first law equation above to find the required quantity.

$\begin{align}
Q &= \Delta U+W \\\\
-140\ \text J&= 144\ \text J+W \\\\
\Rightarrow W&= -284\ \text J
 \end{align}$

The work done by the gas molecules is:

$\boxed{-284}\ \text J$

assessment_items.question_content:
A sample of ideal gas in a cylinder loses $250\ \text J$ of heat while the gas molecules gain $264\ \text J$ of internal energy in the process.

**What is the work done by the gas molecules?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text J$
hints:
##Strategy

One form of the first law of thermodynamics reads

$\underbrace {Q}_{\text{Heat added to the system}}=\Delta U+\underbrace{W}_{\text{Work done by the gas}}$

It's basically saying that when we add heat to a gas, it can either be used for expansion (work done in pushing a piston) or for increasing the temperature/kinetic energy of the gas molecules $(\Delta U)$.

##Solution

Here, 

- Heat is lost by the gas, so $Q=-250\ \text J$

- Internal energy is *gained*, so$\Delta U=264\ \text J$

Now let's input these into the first law equation above to find the required quantity.

$\begin{align}
Q &= \Delta U+W \\\\
-250\ \text J&= 264\ \text J+W \\\\
\Rightarrow W&= -514\ \text J
 \end{align}$

The work done by the gas molecules is:

$\boxed{-514}\ \text J$

assessment_items.question_content:
When a certain amount water changes from liquid to vapor state, the work done by the molecules against the outside pressure is $340\ \text J$. In this process, the heat supplied to the system is $4500\ \text J$.

**What percentage of the total heat goes into increasing the internal energy of the molecules?**  
*Note: Report your answer correct to one decimal place*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\%$
hints:
##Strategy

One form of the first law of thermodynamics reads

$\underbrace {Q}_{\text{Heat added to the system}}=\Delta U+\underbrace{W}_{\text{Work done by the gas}}$

It's basically saying that when we add heat to a gas, it can either be used for expansion (work done in pushing a piston) or for increasing the temperature/kinetic energy of the gas molecules $(\Delta U)$.

Here, we need to find the fraction $\dfrac{\Delta U}{Q}$.

##Solution

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{\Delta U}{Q} &= \dfrac{Q-W}{Q} \\\\
&= 1-\dfrac WQ \\\\
 &= 1-\dfrac{340}{4500}\\\\
&=0.924\\\\
&=92.4\%
\end{align}$

The percentage of energy that goes into increasing the internal energy is:

$\boxed{92.4}\ \%$

assessment_items.question_content:
When a certain amount water changes from liquid to vapor state, the work done by the molecules against the outside pressure is $480\ \text J$. In this process, the heat supplied to the system is $7500\ \text J$.

**What percentage of the total heat goes into increasing the internal energy of the molecules?**  
*Note: Report your answer correct to one decimal place*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\%$
hints:
##Strategy

One form of the first law of thermodynamics reads

$\underbrace {Q}_{\text{Heat added to the system}}=\Delta U+\underbrace{W}_{\text{Work done by the gas}}$

It's basically saying that when we add heat to a gas, it can either be used for expansion (work done in pushing a piston) or for increasing the temperature/kinetic energy of the gas molecules $(\Delta U)$.

Here, we need to find the fraction $\dfrac{\Delta U}{Q}$.

##Solution

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{\Delta U}{Q} &= \dfrac{Q-W}{Q} \\\\
&= 1-\dfrac WQ \\\\
 &= 1-\dfrac{480}{7500}\\\\
&=0.936\\\\
&=93.6\%
\end{align}$

The percentage of energy that goes into increasing the internal energy is:

$\boxed{93.6}\ \%$

assessment_items.question_content:
In a thermodynamic process, a gas releases $30\ \text J$ of heat as $10\ \text J$ of work is done on it. The initial internal energy of the gas is $100\ \text J$.

**Find the final internal energy of the gas.**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text J$
hints:
##Strategy

One form of the first law of thermodynamics reads

$\underbrace {Q}_{\text{Heat added to the system}}=\Delta U+\underbrace{W}_{\text{Work done by the gas}}$

It's basically saying that when we add heat to a gas, it can either be used for expansion (work done in pushing a piston) or for increasing the temperature/kinetic energy of the gas molecules $(\Delta U)$.

##Solution

Here, 

- Heat is released, so $Q=-30\ \text J$

- Work is done *on* the gas, so, $W=-10\ \text J$

Now let's input these into the first law equation above to find the required quantity.

$\begin{align}
Q &= \Delta U+W \\\\
-30\ \text J&= (U_f-U_i)+(-10\ \text J) \\\\
\Rightarrow -20\ \text J&= (U_f-100\ \text J)\\\\
\Rightarrow U_f&=80\ \text J
 \end{align}$

The final internal energy of the gas is:

$\boxed{80}\ \text J$

assessment_items.question_content:
In a thermodynamic process, a gas releases $55\ \text J$ of heat as $12\ \text J$ of work is done on it. The initial internal energy of the gas is $130\ \text J$.

**Find the final internal energy of the gas.**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text J$
hints:
##Strategy

One form of the first law of thermodynamics reads

$\underbrace {Q}_{\text{Heat added to the system}}=\Delta U+\underbrace{W}_{\text{Work done by the gas}}$

It's basically saying that when we add heat to a gas, it can either be used for expansion (work done in pushing a piston) or for increasing the temperature/kinetic energy of the gas molecules $(\Delta U)$.

##Solution

Here, 

- Heat is released, so $Q=-55\ \text J$

- Work is done *on* the gas, so, $W=-12\ \text J$

Now let's input these into the first law equation above to find the required quantity.

$\begin{align}
Q &= \Delta U+W \\\\
-55\ \text J&= (U_f-U_i)+(-12\ \text J) \\\\
\Rightarrow -43\ \text J&= (U_f-130\ \text J)\\\\
\Rightarrow U_f&=87\ \text J
 \end{align}$

The final internal energy of the gas is:

$\boxed{87}\ \text J$

assessment_items.question_content:
An ideal gas absorbs $3\times10^4\ \text J$ of heat and does $9\times10^4\ \text J$ of work to go from an initial to a final state.

The transition from this initial to final state can also be achieved through a second process where the gas releases $10^4\ \text J$ of heat.

**What work needs to be done by the gas in the second process?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\times10^4\ \text J$
hints:
##Strategy

One form of the first law of thermodynamics reads

$\underbrace {Q}_{\text{Heat added to the system}}=\Delta U+\underbrace{W}_{\text{Work done by the gas}}$

It's basically saying that when we add heat to a gas, it can either be used for expansion (work done in pushing a piston) or for increasing the temperature/kinetic energy of the gas molecules $(\Delta U)$.

Now $\Delta U$ depends on only the change in temperature. So in taking a gas from a given initial to final state, the $\Delta U$ will remain same (even though $Q$ and $W$ might differ).

##Solution

We can use the first law equation for the first process and find $\Delta U$. The change in internal energy will be the same for the second process as well, so we can use this $\Delta U$ and the first law equation again for the second process and find the work done.

For the first process,


$\begin{align}
Q &= \Delta U+W \\\\
3\times 10^4\ \text J&= \Delta U+(9\times 10^4\ \text J) \\\\
\Rightarrow \Delta U&= -6\times 10^4\ \text J
 \end{align}$

In the second process, the gas *releases* heat, so $Q$ is negative. Using the $\Delta U$ we got above and the first law, we get

$\begin{align}
Q &= \Delta U+W \\\\
-1\times 10^4\ \text J&=-6\times 10^4\ \text J+W\\\\
\Rightarrow W&= 5\times 10^4\ \text J
 \end{align}$

The work done by the gas in the second process is:

$\boxed 5\times10^4\ \text J$.

assessment_items.question_content:
An ideal gas absorbs $7\times10^5\ \text J$ of heat and does $8\times10^5\ \text J$ of work to go from an initial to a final state.

The transition from this initial to final state can also be achieved through a second process where the gas releases $2\times10^5\ \text J$ of heat.

**What work needs to be done by the gas in the second process?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\times10^5\ \text J$
hints:
##Strategy

One form of the first law of thermodynamics reads

$\underbrace {Q}_{\text{Heat added to the system}}=\Delta U+\underbrace{W}_{\text{Work done by the gas}}$

It's basically saying that when we add heat to a gas, it can either be used for expansion (work done in pushing a piston) or for increasing the temperature/kinetic energy of the gas molecules $(\Delta U)$.

Now $\Delta U$ depends on only the change in temperature. So in taking a gas from a given initial to final state, the $\Delta U$ will remain same (even though $Q$ and $W$ might differ).

##Solution

We can use the first law equation for the first process and find $\Delta U$. The change in internal energy will be the same for the second process as well, so we can use this $\Delta U$ and the first law equation again for the second process and find the work done.

For the first process,


$\begin{align}
Q &= \Delta U+W \\\\
7\times 10^5\ \text J&= \Delta U+(8\times 10^5\ \text J) \\\\
\Rightarrow \Delta U&= -1\times 10^5\ \text J
 \end{align}$

In the second process, the gas *releases* heat, so $Q$ is negative. Using the $\Delta U$ we got above and the first law, we get

$\begin{align}
Q &= \Delta U+W \\\\
-2\times 10^5\ \text J&=-1\times 10^5\ \text J+W\\\\
\Rightarrow W&= -1\times 10^5\ \text J
 \end{align}$

The work done by the gas in the second process is:

$\boxed {-1}\times10^5\ \text J$.
